sourceMovie.title: My Neighbor Totoro
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto My Neighbor Totoro (1988) as a fantasy theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The fantasy and animation blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Spirited Away
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Spirited Away (2001) captures the fantasy appeal of My Neighbor Totoro without simply imitating it. The animation streak running through both films is what puts Spirited Away at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Onward
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy appeal of My Neighbor Totoro — that funny edge, the animation — translates cleanly into Onward (2020). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Kiki's Delivery Service
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Kiki's Delivery Service (1989) clearly understood what makes fantasy work — the same quality that keeps My Neighbor Totoro rewatchable. Expect the same uplifting rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Secret World of Arrietty
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Secret World of Arrietty (2010) is the kind of film My Neighbor Totoro fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The fantasy overlap is real, but it's the uplifting emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: The Boy and the Heron
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Boy and the Heron shares My Neighbor Totoro's gift for fantasy — the 2023 film delivers animation without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Boy and the Beast
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes My Neighbor Totoro compelling — the fantasy, the animation — shows up in The Boy and the Beast (2015) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Mirai
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of My Neighbor Totoro is alive in Mirai (2018), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: A Letter to Momo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A Letter to Momo (2012) operates in the same fantasy as My Neighbor Totoro, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Pokémon: The Rise of Darkrai
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Pokémon: The Rise of Darkrai (2007) belongs to the same conversation as My Neighbor Totoro — both anchor their fantasy in animation emotion that doesn't let go. The uplifting undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Big Hero 6
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If My Neighbor Totoro's animation stayed with you, Big Hero 6 (2014) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The family through-line is the real common thread between them.

faq.question: What should I watch after My Neighbor Totoro?
faq.answer: Try Spirited Away, Onward, Kiki's Delivery Service—each shares DNA with My Neighbor Totoro while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is My Neighbor Totoro?
faq.answer: My Neighbor Totoro (1988) is best described as Fantasy, Animation, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is My Neighbor Totoro worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
